zotero.abstractNote: Legged robots are capable of traversing complex terrains, but achieving robust locomotion remains a challenge. Model-based control methods, such as trajectory optimization (TO) and model predictive control (MPC), can generate high-quality motions but are often sensitive to model inaccuracies and external disturbances. Reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in learning robust control policies, but training from scratch is often sample-inefficient and requires careful reward engineering. In this work, we propose a framework that combines the strengths of TO and RL. We use TO to generate a set of optimal trajectories for a variety of tasks and terrains, and then use these trajectories to guide the RL training process. We show that our approach, which we call TO-guided RL, can learn robust locomotion policies that outperform both pure TO and pure RL methods in terms of success rate and robustness to disturbances. We evaluate our approach on a quadruped robot in simulation and show that it can traverse challenging terrains, including slopes, stairs, and uneven ground, with high success rates.
